narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score and middle-family income background, you've put real effort into your law school application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted a 68% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was unfortunately rejected—our goal is to select students with the strongest likelihood of bar passage success. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which falls well below the typical student's rank of around the 5th or 6th decile and strongly pushed the model toward expecting a bar failure. Your Year 1 decile of 5, closer to average, had a milder but still negative influence in the same direction, suggesting your early promise didn't hold up later on. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 40—higher than the average of about 37—worked significantly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward bar passage success. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0, slightly below the typical 3.2, had only a tiny positive nudge toward passing, while your middle income bracket and full-time student status were minor factors that didn't sway things much either way. Compared to most applicants, where stronger later-year performance is key to bar success signals, your profile stood out for that Year 3 dip despite your standout test score. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income family—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger coursework or retakes, perhaps retaking the LSAT to solidify that strength, and consider programs that support later-year performance. Building a narrative around your resilience could help in reapplying or appealing. You've got strengths to build on—keep pushing, and reach out if you need guidance on next steps. We're rooting for your success.
